text:
I'm seeing occurences of hyphens in CRAN package versions when updating. Is something odd happening? I vaguely recall something on a mailing list (but may have made that up). E.g. updating I see things like
```S7 :
 Version 0.2.1 installed in /usr/local/lib/R/library 
 Version 0.2.1-1 available at <https://cloud.r-project.org>```
Are these real bumps? - I cannot see them on <https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/S7/>. Hope this made sense.

text: There is a new subdirectory `src/contrib/Transit` (which you can view in the browser) and it contains updated packages accommodating the really poorly planned, communicated, or executed last minute changes to the C API of R.

elements.text: There is a new subdirectory

(which you can view in the browser) and it contains updated packages accommodating the really poorly planned, communicated, or executed last minute changes to the C API of R.

text: CRANberries now filters these out, but we have a few fake 'new version' because of that as well as a two fake 'removals' as two (of the then eleven) were actually ... BioConductor packages.

I am also submitting a new version right now to fix this.

text: My personal view:  Having src/contrib/Transit helps CRAN as they "need" an update to Rcpp because of the API changes.  But as I managed release for Rcpp, I have a *strong* and stated preference for properly planned and tested release in January and July.  Not now just because R Core can't announce changes / phases changes in properly.  (At least two previous cycles had such non-release pre-release Rcpp versions: 1.0.8.* and 1.0.13.*.  The current approach, proposed by KH, is better as it makes it clear that 1.1.1-1 not actually a proper release.  _If_ someone wants to test against our release candidates, they are at GH and R-universe, currently at 1.1.1.9.  And we work hard to keep them clean and always workable.  That does not mean I want random forced releases.

text: There is a _obscure feature_ we should use more.  Read `?available.packages` and its Details: section about filters.  I used that before when we tried at $previousWork to cleverly combine several r-universe repos with / without regard to custom branches and mains.  In short, you can inject new 'filters' either via `options(available_package_filters)` or via an argument to `available.packages()`.

obscure feature
we should use more.  Read

and its Details: section about filters.  I used that before when we tried at $previousWork to cleverly combine several r-universe repos with / without regard to custom branches and mains.  In short, you can inject new 'filters' either via
options(available_package_filters)
or via an argument to
available.packages()

text: It is important to NOT use default as an `a.b.c-1' will sort higher than 'a.b.c' and the default set that run includes a 'duplicates' filter.  So 'a.b.c' is gone, and when you then (manually) filter out by `endsWith(ap$Repository, "src/contrib/Transit")` your are left with packages (affecting S7, Rcpp, ... in that directory).  Funsies!   I broke (parts of) one run of r2u that way.  Funny how resiliency works though because packages needing RcppArmadillo brough Rcpp back in via their Depends:.  Full dependency graphs for the win!

endsWith(ap$Repository, "src/contrib/Transit")

your are left with packages (affecting S7, Rcpp, ... in that directory).  Funsies!   I broke (parts of) one run of r2u that way.  Funny how resiliency works though because packages needing RcppArmadillo brough Rcpp back in via their Depends:.  Full dependency graphs for the win!

text: Anyway, the main upshot is it should not 'break' things on r-release or r-devel but it will 'look different' w.r.t version numbers.  Which is what you noticed.

elements.text: Anyway, the main upshot is it should not 'break' things on r-release or r-devel but it will 'look different' w.r.t version numbers.  Which is what you noticed.

text: Longer-term would be nice to realize that testing 'dev' and testing 'release' are not the same thing.  Currently at CRAN they are more or less forced to be the same and that may not be such a great idea.

elements.text: Longer-term would be nice to realize that testing 'dev' and testing 'release' are not the same thing.  Currently at CRAN they are more or less forced to be the same and that may not be such a great idea.

text: Hi, materializing here. cran2copr doesn't get updates from this transitory versions. Those are put there by CRAN as Dirk explained just to get rid of NOTEs and WARNINGs on CRAN due to API changes. But we don't care about those as long as packages build and work. And that's still true for current releases.

elements.text: Hi, materializing here. cran2copr doesn't get updates from this transitory versions. Those are put there by CRAN as Dirk explained just to get rid of NOTEs and WARNINGs on CRAN due to API changes. But we don't care about those as long as packages build and work. And that's still true for current releases.

text: Out of nerdy curiousity, how do you filter them, and how come that was already working?   Because if we drop into a random container, or use a random R installation anywhere, and ask for `install.packages("Rcpp")` (from source, say) then we get 1.1.1-1 (for the reasons outlines above concerning the default 'filters')  which you and I as parts of Team Rcpp say with a straight face 'does not exist' ?   I.e. what magic did cran2copr have to not fall for that (when CRANberries did) ?

text: I could be that you already group by package and take `head(1)` so that a default of `src/contrib` works.  We have had 'n+1' subdirectories below `src/contrib` forever but those are excluded usually via the 'package needs to match the R version requesting' and they are usually set to n+1 or n+2.

For completeness, this too is critical because otherwise you would not get multiple entries per package: the default 'duplicates' filter is turned off via this line:
<https://github.com/cran4linux/cran2copr/blob/d15b0e2886ec9c3cdb3023d71ef3391c667cc5bf/config.r#L13>
